narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring so much into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—just over 54% chance of not passing. Picture it like a story the model pieced together from your profile: it started with your school performance, where your Year 3 decile of 4 stood out as the biggest red flag, signaling to the model that your grades had settled into the lower half of your peers by the end, pulling hardest toward that failure prediction. Layered on top was your Year 1 decile of 5, which didn't show the early spark of top-tier promise the model favors for bar success, making those early and later rankings the top two chapters in this cautious tale. Your upper-middle family income added another layer of concern, as the model associates it with patterns where students sometimes face unique pressures that haven't historically led to bar passage as reliably. Even with a solid LSAT of 36 and undergrad GPA of 3.4—numbers that are respectable—they weren't standout enough to counterbalance, ranking as the next strongest influences tilting things toward risk. As a white female going full-time, those details rounded out the picture in ways the model linked to slightly higher hurdles in its past stories of bar outcomes. It's not that any one piece doomed you, but together, they painted a profile where the model couldn't confidently bet on passage, leading to this tough call on your application. The good news? This is just one snapshot—your target law passion shines through, and with targeted boosts like climbing those deciles in future steps, you could rewrite this story entirely. Hang in there; you've got the drive to prove it wrong.
